content.text:
Confirmed. The tests never needed a `<Wrapper>` because the `vi.mock('@mantine/core', …)` factory swaps out every Mantine component the file uses for a plain-div stub — no real Mantine rendering happens, so `MantineProvider` was never required.

Your edit swapped `Container` (which the mock stubs at [CustomAppShell.vitest.spec.tsx:45](src/components/CustomAppShell/CustomAppShell.vitest.spec.tsx#L45)) for `Stack`, which isn't in the mock's override list. The `...actual` spread means `Stack` falls through to the *real* Mantine `Stack`, and the real `Stack` calls `useMantineTheme()` — hence the missing-provider error.

Fix: add a `Stack` stub to the mock alongside the others.

content.text: What's the best approach here? Components are tested individually so is it most straightforward to have a global, universal way to mock all of Mantine?

A universal mock isn't the direction I'd go — it just moves this same bug to a bigger, shared blast radius (every test now depends on one file staying in sync with every Mantine component anyone uses, including ones like `Stack` you'll add later). The project's own convention already flags the better fix: [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) says not to rely on Mantine internals in tests, so the real answer is to stop stubbing Mantine components at all and instead use a shared `render` wrapper (a `test-utils.tsx` with `MantineProvider`) plus real accessible queries (`getByRole`, `getByLabelText`) — that's what this test *should* be doing per your own conventions anyway, rather than asserting on a mocked `data-testid="app-shell"`.

Tradeoff: real Mantine renders are marginally slower and you lose the ability to trivially inspect internal props like `navbar.collapsed.mobile` — but you gain tests that actually verify what a user sees, and you eliminate this whole class of "forgot to stub a component" failures for good.

Want me to migrate this test file (and check how many others use the same per-file mock pattern) to a shared `MantineProvider` wrapper?

content.text:
Confirmed in the installed `@mantine/core` types (not `Container` itself, despite the name): **`Grid`** and **`SimpleGrid`** both accept `type?: 'media' | 'container'` (default `'media'`), switching their responsive breakpoints from viewport media queries to CSS container queries.

- [Grid.d.ts](node_modules/@mantine/core/lib/components/Grid/Grid.d.ts) — `type="container"` makes `Grid.Col` spans respond to the size of the `Grid`'s container rather than the viewport.
- [SimpleGrid.d.ts](node_modules/@mantine/core/lib/components/SimpleGrid/SimpleGrid.d.ts) — same option, same effect on its column count breakpoints.

No other component in this version exposes a `type` prop with a `'container'` value (the `Scroller`/`Grid` context hits above are just `stylesApi` class names, unrelated).

content.text:
That's a plain CSS containment concern, not a Mantine one — Mantine's `Grid`/`SimpleGrid` `type="container"` only sets `container-type` on their own internal wrapper for their own column breakpoints; it doesn't expose `cqw` to arbitrary children. To make *any* element a query container yourself, you set the CSS `container-type` property (and optionally `container-name`) on it:

```css
.container {
  container-type: inline-size;   /* or 'size' if you also need cqh/height queries */
  container-name: card;          /* optional — scopes @container queries by name */
}
```

Descendants can then use `cqw`/`cqi` (and `cqh`/`cqb`, `cqmin`, `cqmax` if `size`) in any length value, or scope rules with `@container (min-width: 400px) { ... }` / `@container card (min-width: 400px) { ... }`.

Since there's no Mantine style prop for `containerType` (confirmed above — it's not in `Box`'s style props), this is the raw-CSS escape hatch your [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) convention allows. In this codebase that means either a CSS module class, or the `style` prop directly if it's one-off:

```tsx
<Box style={{ containerType: 'inline-size' }}>
  <Text fz="6cqw">Scales with container width</Text>
</Box>
```

92	/**
93	 * Base class for typed domain/application errors.
94	 *
95	 * This class standardises:
96	 * - a machine-readable error code;
97	 * - expected vs unexpected classification;
98	 * - a safe public-facing message;
99	 * - optional typed details payload.

102	 * @template TDetails Optional typed payload containing structured context.
103	 */
104	export class AppError<TCode extends AppErrorCode = AppErrorCode, TDetails = unknown> extends Error {
105	    /**
106	     * Stable machine-readable code used for classification and monitoring.
107	     */
108	    readonly code: TCode;
109	    /**
110	     * Marks whether this error is expected during normal operation.
111	     */
112	    readonly expected: boolean;
113	    /**
114	     * Optional strongly typed details payload for structured context.
115	     */
116	    readonly details?: TDetails;
117	    /**
118	     * Safe message intended to be shown to end users.
119	     */
120	    readonly publicMessage: string;
121	
122	    /**
123	     * Creates a typed application error.
124	     *
125	     * @param init Initialiser containing code, message, and optional metadata.
126	     */
127	    constructor(init: AppErrorInit<TCode, TDetails>) {
128	        super(init.message, { cause: init.cause });
129	        this.name = new.target.name;
130	        this.code = init.code;
131	        this.expected = init.expected ?? true;
132	        this.details = init.details;
133	        this.publicMessage = init.publicMessage ?? DEFAULT_PUBLIC_ERROR_MESSAGE;
134	        Object.setPrototypeOf(this, new.target.prototype);
135	    }

284	/**
285	 * Normalizes an unknown error into a standardized AppError instance.
286	 *
287	 * Handles various error types and converts them to appropriate AppError subclasses:
288	 * - AppError instances are returned as-is
289	 * - ZodError instances are converted to ValidationError
290	 * - Standard Error instances are converted to InternalError
291	 * - Non-Error values are wrapped in InternalError
292	 *
293	 * @template TDetails - The type of additional error details
294	 * @param error - The unknown error value to normalize
295	 * @param options - Configuration options for error normalization
296	 * @param options.message - Custom error message to use instead of the original
297	 * @param options.details - Additional details to attach to the normalized error
298	 * @param options.publicMessage - Public-facing message safe to display to users
299	 * @returns A normalized AppError instance with consistent structure and properties
300	 */
301	export const normalizeUnknownError = <TDetails = unknown>(
302	    error: unknown,
303	    options: NormalizeUnknownErrorOptions<TDetails> = {},
304	): AppError => {
305	    if (error instanceof AppError) {
306	        return error as AppError<AppErrorCode, unknown>;
307	    }
308	
309	    if (error instanceof ZodError) {
310	        return new ValidationError(
311	            options.message ?? 'Validation failed.',
312	            {
313	                cause: error,
314	                details: options.details,
315	                publicMessage: options.publicMessage,
316	            },
317	        );
318	    }
319	
320	    if (error instanceof Error) {
321	        return new InternalError(
322	            options.message ?? error.message,
323	            {
324	                cause: error,
325	                details: options.details,
326	                publicMessage: options.publicMessage,
327	            },
328	        );
329	    }
330	
331	    return new InternalError(
332	        options.message ?? 'Non-Error value thrown.',
333	        {
334	            cause: error,
335	            details: options.details,
336	            publicMessage: options.publicMessage,
337	        },
338	    );
339	};

341	/**
342	 * Type guard that narrows unknown values to {@link AppError}.
343	 *
344	 * @param error Unknown value to test.
345	 * @returns `true` when the value is an AppError instance.
346	 */
347	export const isAppError = (error: unknown): error is AppError =>
348	    error instanceof AppError;
349	
350	/**
351	 * Determines whether an error should be treated as expected.
352	 *
353	 * Classification rules:
354	 * - {@link AppError}: defer to its `expected` flag.
355	 * - {@link ZodError}: always expected (validation failures).
356	 * - Any other error/value: unexpected.
357	 *
358	 * @param error Unknown value to classify.
359	 * @returns `true` when the error is expected; otherwise `false`.
360	 */
361	export const isExpectedError = (error: unknown): boolean => {
362	    if (error instanceof AppError) {
363	        return error.expected;
364	    }
365	
366	    if (error instanceof ZodError) {
367	        return true;
368	    }
369	
370	    return false;
371	};

373	/**
374	 * Returns a safe user-facing message for any unknown error.
375	 *
376	 * Message resolution order:
377	 * 1. {@link AppError.publicMessage}
378	 * 2. Generic validation message for {@link ZodError}
379	 * 3. Caller-provided fallback
380	 *
381	 * @param error Unknown value to transform.
382	 * @param fallback Message used when no richer public message is available.
383	 * @returns A safe string suitable for UI/API consumers.
384	 */
385	export const toPublicMessage = (
386	    error: unknown,
387	    fallback = DEFAULT_PUBLIC_ERROR_MESSAGE,
388	): string => {
389	    if (error instanceof AppError) {
390	        return error.publicMessage;
391	    }
392	
393	    if (error instanceof ZodError) {
394	        return 'Invalid request.';
395	    }
396	
397	    return fallback;
398	};

content.text: Found it — React discards the first (concurrent) render attempt on error and retries synchronously before invoking the error boundary; `mockReturnValueOnce` only covers the first attempt, so the retry gets the default (working) theme and renders fine. Switching to `mockReturnValue` (persistent for both attempts) and cleaning up the debug line.

Adding an `export {}` to make it a module fixes it.

Done. Moved the `MantineThemeOther` augmentation to [src/types/mantine.d.ts](src/types/mantine.d.ts), following this repo's existing convention of ambient `.d.ts` files living in `src/types/` (alongside `images.d.ts`, `supertest.d.ts`).

One subtlety worth flagging: a bare `declare module 'x' { ... }` in a `.d.ts` file with no top-level `import`/`export` is treated by TypeScript as a global script, so instead of *merging* with `@mantine/core`'s real types it *replaced* them — every Mantine import in the codebase broke with "has no exported member". Fixed by adding `export {};` at the top, which forces the file into module mode so the declaration merges correctly. [theme.ts](src/theme.ts) now only contains the `createTheme()` call. `typecheck`, `lint`, and `knip` all pass, and the `CustomAppShell` tests still pass (8/8).

content.text:
Good — this is now the single canonical default, set once in `beforeEach`. The mock factory just declares a bare `vi.fn()` with no baked-in implementation, so there's no longer two places defining the same theme shape.

Summary of the change:
- [CustomAppShell.vitest.spec.tsx:79](src/components/CustomAppShell/CustomAppShell.vitest.spec.tsx#L79) — mock factory's `useMantineTheme` is now a bare `vi.fn()`; the working theme shape is defined exactly once, in `beforeEach` at [line 89](src/components/CustomAppShell/CustomAppShell.vitest.spec.tsx#L89).
- The two "throws" tests now use `mockReturnValueOnce` (chained twice) instead of the persistent `mockReturnValue`, making it explicit that only *those* tests need a different theme shape, and only for the duration of the render — no manual restore needed since the queue drains back to whatever `beforeEach` set.
- Kept the double-queued `mockReturnValueOnce` (not a single one) with a comment explaining why: React discards a failed concurrent render and retries synchronously before handing off to the error boundary, so a single queued value gets consumed by the discarded attempt and the real attempt would see the correct default and not throw — this was verified flaky-free across 5 repeated runs.

`typecheck`, `lint`, `knip`, and all 9 tests pass.
